title: The Mystery of the Missing Mascot

narrative.intro: The school mascot costume has vanished from the gym storage room — right before the big pep rally! Principal Reeves has called in the Logic Bureau (that's you) to investigate. You'll review interviews, texts, emails, and social media posts. Everyone's got a theory — your job is to find the reasoning that doesn't hold up.
narrative.outro: Thanks to your sharp eye for bad reasoning, the costume was found stuffed behind the bleachers. The real story? A student borrowed it for a TikTok and forgot to return it. Case closed, detective.

cases.briefing: Tommy was near the gym at lunch. His account has some useful details — but one of his conclusions doesn't follow from the facts.
cases.speaker: Tommy Chen
claims: I was shooting hoops near the gym at lunch, so I had a clear view of the door.
I saw Jake come out of the storage room around 12:10 carrying something bulky.
Jake got detention twice last month for being disruptive, so he's the type who would steal something.
The gym door locks automatically — I know because I got locked out last week.
options: Getting detention for being disruptive doesn't connect to stealing — those are different behaviors.
Tommy might not have been close enough to actually see what Jake was carrying.
Jake coming out of the storage room is already suspicious enough without the detention detail.
cases.explanation: Tommy saw something genuinely useful (Jake leaving with a bulky item), but then uses Jake's detention history — for being disruptive, not stealing — as evidence he's a thief. Past behavior in one area doesn't prove guilt in a completely different area. That's Attacking the Person: using someone's character to substitute for actual evidence.

cases.briefing: Mia texted the student council group. She did some real investigating — but then makes one leap she shouldn't.
cases.speaker: Mia Park
claims: I checked the sign-out sheet and only 4 people had gym keys yesterday: Coach Davis, Ms. Lin, the cheer captain, and the drama president
I talked to Coach Davis and Ms. Lin — both say they locked up after use
That leaves the cheer captain and drama president. I surveyed 30 students and 26 of them think the drama president is more likely
So we should start our investigation with the drama president
options: Student opinions about who's 'more likely' aren't evidence — they're guesses based on gut feeling.
Mia should have interviewed the cheer captain and drama president directly instead of surveying.
The sign-out sheet might be incomplete — other people could have unofficial access.
cases.explanation: Mia did great detective work narrowing suspects to two people. But then instead of investigating both, she polls students about who seems more suspicious. Student opinions aren't evidence — they're just popularity-based guesses. Using 'most people think so' as a reason to target someone is the Bandwagon fallacy.

cases.briefing: Coach Davis emailed the principal with his assessment. He raises valid concerns — but his conclusion forces a choice that isn't necessary.
cases.speaker: Coach Davis
claims: I checked the storage room lock and it shows no signs of forced entry.
Only staff and two student leaders have authorized keys to that room.
We need to either suspend key privileges for all students immediately, or accept that this will keep happening.
I recommend suspending privileges effective today.
options: There are other responses besides 'suspend all privileges' or 'do nothing' — like adding a sign-out log or cameras.
The coach is overreacting by wanting to suspend privileges before the investigation is complete.
No forced entry doesn't rule out someone propping the door open or getting a key copied.
cases.explanation: Coach Davis frames it as suspend-or-suffer, but those aren't the only options. The school could add security cameras, require a sign-out log, change the locks, or pair students with staff. Presenting an extreme action and inaction as the only two choices — ignoring all the middle ground — is the False Dilemma.

cases.briefing: Vince documented the timeline on his account. His facts might be right, but watch how he connects them...
cases.speaker: ViralVince
claims: ok so I've been investigating 🔍 the drama club had rehearsal in the gym from 3-5pm yesterday
the costume was confirmed still in storage at 5pm when Ms. Lin locked up
the basketball team had practice from 6-8pm and used the storage room for equipment
basketball practice was the last activity before the costume went missing, so someone on the team took it during practice
options: Being the last group in the gym doesn't mean the basketball team took it — someone could have come after practice.
Vince should have asked basketball players directly instead of posting accusations.
The costume might have already been taken before basketball practice started.
cases.explanation: Vince builds a real timeline, which is good detective work. But his conclusion — that because basketball was the last known activity, the team must be responsible — doesn't follow. Someone could have entered after practice, during cleanup, or even early morning. Being last in a timeline doesn't equal guilt. That's the 'After ≠ Because' fallacy.

cases.briefing: Mr. Gonzalez left his observations for the principal. He noticed a real clue — but then makes a connection that's too big of a jump.
cases.speaker: Mr. Gonzalez
claims: Found muddy footprints near the bleachers when I cleaned Tuesday evening
The footprints were small — probably size 5 or 6, so likely a younger student
Two weeks ago I caught a 5th grader hiding a skateboard behind those same bleachers
The footprints are small and near the bleachers — this has to be another 5th grader doing something they shouldn't
options: One 5th grader hiding a skateboard doesn't mean every incident near the bleachers involves a 5th grader.
Small footprints could belong to anyone, not just younger students.
The muddy footprints might have nothing to do with the missing costume.
cases.explanation: Mr. Gonzalez found a real clue (small footprints near the bleachers). But he connects it to a completely separate incident involving a different 5th grader and a skateboard — then concludes this must also be a 5th grader. One past incident doesn't establish a pattern. That's Jumping to Conclusions from too little evidence.

cases.briefing: The VP's official response looks thorough and professional. But somewhere in her analysis, she slides away from the actual question.
cases.speaker: VP Rodriguez
claims: Security footage from the east hallway shows three students near the gym between 12:00 and 12:30.
We have identified the students and will be conducting interviews this afternoon.
This incident highlights a broader concern: our school's annual security budget has not increased since 2019, despite enrollment growing 15%.
I will be requesting an emergency budget review at Thursday's board meeting to address facility security.
options: The security budget discussion is a real concern but doesn't help solve who took the costume — it's a different topic.
The VP shouldn't use a single incident to justify requesting more budget.
Identifying three students from the footage is the most important lead and should be the focus.
A 15% enrollment increase doesn't necessarily mean security needs have grown proportionally.
cases.explanation: VP Rodriguez starts strong — real footage, identified suspects, interviews planned. Then she pivots to budget advocacy, which is a legitimate but completely separate conversation. The budget didn't cause the theft, and fixing the budget won't solve who did it. Slipping from 'who took the costume' to 'we need more money' is a Red Herring — changing the subject mid-argument.
